---
title: "Import Data"
---

More than a thousand LIS and LWS datasets are available for use and analysis within LISSY. Learning how to import these datasets is one of the first key steps in the user workflow, as it requires becoming familiar with the built-in tools specifically designed for this purpose.

Before getting started, there are a few important concepts to keep in mind:

- LIS and LWS datasets cannot be accessed simultaneously (remember to select the appropriate **Project** from the dropdown menu in the user interface).

- Datasets are organized by country and year, following the `ccyy` naming convention (e.g., `de18`, `de19`).

- In addition, each `ccyy` dataset is divided by level:

  - household-level data
  - individual-level data

R users have two different methods available to import data into LISSY:

1.  The newer `lissyrtools::lissyuse()` function

2.  The `read.LIS()` method, which is still used in some cases, particularly for less frequently used files such as LWS replicate weights

For the remainder of this guide, we will focus on **Method 1**, which imports the selected LIS datasets into a list, where each element corresponds to a specific country–year dataset.

<br>

# Method 1: `lissyuse()`  {#import_data_r}

Using the `lissyuse()` function from our in-house [lissyrtools](https://lis-cross-national-data-center.github.io/lissyrtools/) package has two main advantages:

1.  Imports all datasets available, given specific ISO-2 country codes by default;

2.  Automatically selects the correct data level based on the variables requested;

```{r, echo=TRUE , message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
```

<br>

## Advantage 1: Import entire country series

At the time we are writting this guide, LIS contains 26 datasets on Italy. This means that if the user wanted to import all years it would have needed to manually specify 26 distinct datasets. On top of it, it still had to know and confirm the exact specific years that we have data for each one of the countries.

```{stata, eval =FALSE, echo=TRUE}
* it22 it20 it16 it14 it12 ...
```

To bypass this problem, `lissyuse()` allows users to import LIS data by specifying the countries they wish to work with via their ISO-2 country codes, as well as optionally filtering by a desired year range.

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}
lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c('it'), 
  vars = "dhi"
)
 
class(lis_data)
names(lis_data)
class(lis_data[[1]])
```

::: callout-important
The output of `lissyrtools::lissyuse()` will always be a **list** of data frames. Each `ccyy` is a different data frame.
:::

To filter the datasets to a shorter time span, the user can also specify the `from` and `to` arguments.

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }
lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("mx", "us"), 
  vars = "dhi",
  from = 2014,
  to = 2016
)
```

Specific datasets can also be selected by specifying its `ccyy` code

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }
lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("it16", "mx", "us"), 
  vars = "dhi",
  from = 2014,
  to = 2016
)
```

<br>

## Advantage 2: Automatic Data-Level Selection

The second benefit of using `lissyuse()` is that it automatically imports datasets at the level determined by the variables selected. If the users selects only household-level varaibles in the `vars` argument such as: disposable household income (<b>dhi</b>), geographic region (<b>region_c</b>) or capital income (<b>hicapital</b>), the data will be loadad at the household level. As soon as the user includes an individual-level variable, such as gender (<b>sex</b>) or labour force status (<b>lfs</b>), the dataset level adjusts accordingly.

To help users identify the imported data level, a message is printed each time `lissyuse()` is executed. In addition, the names of the datasets include an `h` suffix when household-level data is loaded.

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}
h_level_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("dhi", "region_c", "typehh") # only h-level 
)
```

In the example below, only individual-level variables are specified. In such cases, in addition to the printed message indicating the data level, the names of the data frames will contain a `p` suffix.

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }
p_level_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("age", "sex", "relation", "emp", "pi11")
)
```

If both household-level and individual-level variables are specified in `vars`, no suffix is added to the name of the data frame.

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}
merged_hhd_ind_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("dhi", "region_c", "typehh", "age", "sex", "relation", "emp", "pi11")
)

names(merged_hhd_ind_data[[1]])
```

::: callout-important
**Recap:** As soon as a indiviudal-level variable is selected in the argument `vars`, the data will be loaded at individual level as well. If only household-level variables are selected, then data will be loaded at inidividual level. If none are selected, then all variables are considered and consequently data will be at the individual-level.
:::

::: callout-tip
Note as well that **some variables were imported by default**. Variables such as IDs, weights, and others do not need to be defined explicitly, as they are automatically included.
:::

<br>

## Importing LWS data

To import LWS data, one would need to change the selection in the <b>Project</b> drop-down menu, and set the `lws` logical argument equal to `TRUE`.

![](/images/project_lws.png){fig-align="center"}

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}
us_lws_data = lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("us"), 
  vars = c("dnw"), # disposable net wealth - LWS specific variable. 
  from = 2015, 
  lws = TRUE  # `lws` logical argument        
)

names(us_lws_data[[1]])
```
